title: Git Fundamentals
learningObjectives: Understand distributed version control vs centralised systems like SVN
Know the three local areas: working tree, staging index, and repository
Use git init, clone, add, commit, status, log, and diff
Understand commits as snapshots rather than diffs
Understand SHA-1 object IDs and what they identify

theory.content:
What is Git?

Git is a distributed version control system (DVCS) created by Linus Torvalds in 2005. Unlike centralised systems such as SVN or CVS — where there is one authoritative server and clients check out snapshots — in Git every clone is a full copy of the entire history. This means you can commit, branch, and inspect history entirely offline, and there is no single point of failure.

The current stable release is Git 2.50+. Git 2.49, released March 2025, is the N-1 release and introduced several notable improvements:

  • Reftable backend: Now the default format for repos created with --ref-format=reftable. Reftable stores refs in a sorted, indexed binary file — dramatically faster lookups when a repository has thousands of branches or tags.
  • git refs command: A new first-class command for scripting ref listings, replacing many ad-hoc uses of git for-each-ref.
  • git bundle create --stdin: The bundle command now accepts the object list from standard input, enabling more flexible pipeline-based workflows.
  • Large monorepo performance improvements: Reduced overhead in pack-object path and improved partial clone filters.

The Three Areas

Understanding Git's three local areas is essential to understanding every Git command:

  • Working Directory (also called the working tree): The files you actually edit on disk. A file here can be untracked, modified, or unmodified relative to the last commit.
  • Staging Area (also called the Index): A preparation zone. Running git add copies the current state of a file into the index, marking it for inclusion in the next commit. This lets you craft precise commits even when you have changed many files.
  • Repository (the .git directory): The permanent object store. Running git commit takes everything in the index and writes an immutable snapshot — a commit object — into the repository.

Essential Commands

The table below covers the commands you will use in nearly every Git session:

CommandWhat it does
git initCreates a new empty repository (adds a .git directory) in the current folder
git clone <url>Downloads a remote repository and sets up origin tracking automatically
git statusShows the state of the working tree and staging area — which files are modified, staged, or untracked
git add <file>Stages changes (copies file contents into the index for the next commit)
git commit -m "msg"Saves a permanent snapshot of everything in the index into the repository
git logShows the commit history — SHA-1 hashes, authors, dates, and messages
git diffShows changes in the working directory that are not yet staged
git diff --stagedShows changes that are staged (in the index) but not yet committed

Anatomy of a Commit

Each commit in Git is identified by a SHA-1 hash — a 40-character hexadecimal string (e.g., a1b2c3d4e5f6...) derived from the commit's content. Because the hash is content-addressed, the same content always produces the same hash, and any change produces a completely different hash — making accidental data corruption detectable.

A commit object stores:

  • Tree object: a pointer to the root directory snapshot (itself pointing to blob objects for each file)
  • Parent commit(s): the SHA-1(s) of the previous commit(s) — zero for the initial commit, two for a merge commit
  • Author: name, email, and timestamp of who wrote the changes
  • Committer: name, email, and timestamp of who applied the commit (may differ from author in patch-based workflows)
  • Commit message: a human-readable description of why the change was made

Inspect full commit metadata with:

git log --pretty=fuller

commit a1b2c3d4e5f67890abcdef1234567890abcdef12
Author:     Alice Dev <alice@example.com>
AuthorDate: Wed Apr 23 10:15:00 2025 +0000
Commit:     Alice Dev <alice@example.com>
CommitDate: Wed Apr 23 10:15:00 2025 +0000

    Initial commit

    Added README with project overview.

What is New in Git 2.49?

  • Reftable format (new default): Repos initialised with --ref-format=reftable use a compact binary format for ref storage. Ref lookups on repositories with thousands of branches or tags are significantly faster compared to the traditional loose-file format in .git/refs/.
  • git refs command: A dedicated, stable command for listing and filtering refs in scripts — a cleaner alternative to git for-each-ref for common cases.
  • git bundle create --stdin: The bundle creation command now reads the list of objects/revisions from standard input, enabling more composable shell pipelines for offline transfer workflows.
  • Monorepo performance: Improvements to pack-object negotiation and partial clone filter handling reduce CPU and I/O overhead in large repositories.
